course_description:
Students will be introduced to the fundamentals of sustainable agricultural practices and investigate how the intensification of agricultural practices can impact on rural landscapes and the wider environment. Through a series of lectures, discussions, readings, workshops and a field visit to an organic farm (in Australia), students will be exposed to the extremes of intensive modern agriculture in developed countries and to subsistence farming practices in developing countries. In class, students will work through a series of cases studies that examine the interactions, and the often conflicting goals, between agriculture and conservation and learn how these problems can be solved.

The course will focus on sustainable agriculture, in particular the constraints faced by subsistence farmers in developing countries, and the interface with conservation.

They will consider the pressures that abiotic and biotic threats to farming systems exert on rural livelihoods and ﾠinvestigate the threats that invasive pest species pose to local agriculture, the ﾠeffects that this can have on biodiversity and devise strategies to help subsistence farming communities deal with the dual constraints of ongoing food security while caring for and preserving their natural environment and resources.ﾠ

‘ The course has a field component scheduled for 12th March (8am - 3pm). The field trip will not incur any additional costs

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
restrictions: Master of Conservation Biology/Science students only

Additional timetable information
Field Trip will take place in Week 3 - Thursday 12 March 2026
course_aims: Introduce students to the fundamentals of sustainable agricultural practices and investigate how the intensification of agricultural practices can impact on rural landscapes and the wider environment in both developed and developing countries. The course will also investigate how locally managed sustainable methods of harvesting can be built into conservation programmes for improved livelihoods in subsistence farming communities.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the fundamentals of sustainable agricultural practices
Understand how intensification of agricultural practices can impact on rural landscapes and the wider environment.

Understand roles and priorities of stakeholders (farmers; conservationists; local, state and national governments; international and local NGOs) working at the interface of conservation and agriculture
Identify and develop a proposal to address a legitimate conservation problem

assessment_summary.title: Written concept note and oral discussion

assessment_details.title: Written concept note and oral discussion
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Each student will write a concept note. The concept note will be evaluated and then each student will attend an individual 20-minute meeting with course academics to receive oral feedback on the written work. In the meeting students will respond to questions about the rationale for the concept note and will explain why their approach is appropriate for the problem that they have identified. The student and academics will also discuss how the project proposal should be further developed for the oral presentation. This discussion will be scheduled 1 -3 days following your submission of the concept note.Times for meetings will be finalised well in advance. Assessment and marking criteria (for the written concept note and the discussion) will be discussed in class and posted to Blackboard. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Concept note submitted through Turnitin on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
There is a maximum 7 days extension for the submission of this assessment as the feedback from the discussion sessions is required to complete Assessment #2: 'Presentation of research proposal'.
You may be able to apply for an extension for the Concept Note submission of this assessment, however you are not eligible for an extension to the Discussion component. The discussion component will take place 1-3 days following submission. You may only be eligible for rescheduling if exceptional circumstances arise on the day of the scheduled discussion. Please contact your course coordinator with supporting documentation of the exceptional circumstances that prevented you from attending for consideration of an alternate arrangement.
If you have an approved extension you will need to reach out to the course coordinator to confirm your scheduled discussion time.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date. For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Peer assessment factor, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Oral presentation (accompanied by slides) of research proposal. Presentations will take place at previously agreed times (some will need to be outside of time-tabled periods) 18-22nd May in teaching week 12. Students will be divided into groups and all students in a group will attend all presentations by other students in that group. There will be a short Q&A after each presentation. Online submission by Turnitin only by the due date and time. Refer to Blackboard for the submission link. No hard copy or assignment cover sheets are required. Submission via email is not accepted.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
An approved extension to this assessment is only on submission of the Presentation slides. Your Presentation will still take place 1-3 days following submission. If you have an approved extension you will need to reach out to the course coordinator to confirm your scheduled presentation time.
You may only be eligible for rescheduling your presentation if exceptional circumstances arise on the day of the scheduled presentation. Please contact your course coordinator with supporting documentation of the exceptional circumstances that prevented you from attending for consideration of an alternate arrangement.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).
